proposals.title: TraceState
proposals.one_liner: A types-first TS library where invalid agent state transitions and tool-call shapes become red squiggles, not 3am Sentry alerts — with a `tracestate dev` CLI that renders the trace inline in your terminal.
proposals.problem: Agent loops today are `while(true)` spaghetti or fat runtime frameworks. Either way, 'planner called a tool that doesn't exist in this phase' or 'reflection ran before any tool-call produced output' are runtime crashes that surface in production. The Vercel AI SDK and Anthropic SDK both expose tool definitions as typed objects, but nothing wires them into a state machine where the type system enforces which tools are callable from which phase, or which transitions are legal. Existing options force a tradeoff: XState is generic and not SDK-aware; LangGraph.js added compile-time edge validation but is still framework-shaped; Mastra is a runtime. There is no zero-runtime, SDK-native types-first option.
proposals.who_for: Senior TypeScript engineers building production agents on Vercel AI SDK or Anthropic SDK who already write `as const` everywhere, got burned by an agent calling a tool out of order, and want a migration path from their existing while-loop without rewriting it.
proposals.mvp_scope: OSS npm package, zero runtime deps in the core (CLI dependencies isolated to a separate package). Core (`tracestate`): a `defineAgent({ states, transitions, tools })` builder where `states` is a const tuple, `transitions` is a const map of `from -> to[]`, and `tools` is inferred from a Vercel AI SDK or Anthropic SDK tool definitions object. The Vercel AI SDK adapter is the default export (`tracestate/vercel`); Anthropic adapter is a peer with explicitly secondary README hierarchy. The returned `Agent` type exposes a `.step(state, event)` method whose signature only accepts events legal from `state` and whose return type narrows to legal next states. Includes a thin runtime executor (~200 LOC) that drives the loop AND a runtime guard that actually rejects illegal transitions at execution time — the runtime guard is built and proven first via a recorded terminal session, types layered on top so the guarantee is operational, not decorative. Ships with: (a) `createStates([...] as const)` helper that returns a branded union to avoid stringly-typed surfaces — promoted to README hero export, the entry point that earns the click; (b) a `tracestate.config.ts` convention (similar to `next.config.ts`) that declares states and adapters once, read by both the type system and the CLI; (c) a `wrapLegacyLoop(existingFn)` migration helper, scoped to its own tracked workstream with its own done criteria; (d) a separate `@tracestate/dev` package with its own version line, providing a CLI that reads the emitted JSON trace and renders an ASCII/terminal-ink state diagram inline (zero infra, zero SaaS). The split keeps the core's 'zero runtime deps' claim unconditionally true; (e) a README day-one comparison table vs XState, LangGraph.js, and Mastra with a 'last verified against' date stamp per row and a maintenance-policy commitment; (f) a numbered 'time-to-first-squiggle' benchmark in the README — lines of config before the type system catches an illegal transition on a realistic two-phase agent example; (g) a 30-second terminal recording (GIF) of `tracestate dev` rendering a live agent run with a red flash on illegal transition, as the README hero asset. No hosted visualizer in v1; that is documented as a v2 paid SKU but not pre-announced as a feature on the marketing surface.

proposals.title: AgentGraph
proposals.one_liner: A TS-native agent state machine with type-safe transitions, tool-call results as first-class typed events, and a local-only trace replay UI — the LangGraph alternative for TypeScript teams.
proposals.problem: When an agent goes off the rails in prod, you get a 40-message JSON blob and have to mentally simulate the loop to find where it diverged. LangGraph has visualization but is Python-first and runtime-typed. Inngest has observability but no type-level transition guarantees. There is no TS-native tool that gives you both: compile-time legality of transitions AND a visual replay of real production runs against the declared graph.
proposals.who_for: TS agent teams (2-10 engineers) running agents in production who currently grep JSON logs to debug why their agent looped or stalled. Same crowd that adopted XState's hosted visualizer in 2021-2023 and is now evaluating LangGraph but hesitating because their stack is TS, not Python.
proposals.mvp_scope: Single OSS npm package — no hosted SaaS in v1. Three deliverables: (1) `agentgraph` core: a state-machine builder where states are agent phases, events are tool-call results / model responses, and `assign` actions are constrained by the schema. The `defineAgent({ tools, phases })` API takes the EXACT same Zod schemas already used by the Vercel AI SDK or Anthropic SDK tool definitions — not a re-declaration. Event types on transitions infer directly from those schemas with no separate type annotation: zero new type annotations, all inference. README replaces 'XState v5 actors' framing with a three-row comparison table (AgentGraph vs LangGraph.js vs raw XState; columns: TS-native types, SDK-aware tool schemas, local replay) and a 'this is not XState' delta section spelling out the differences explicitly. (2) A versioned JSON trace schema, published as a STANDALONE GitHub repo with its own semver tag BEFORE the npm package ships — citable artifact, schema-stable before any tooling couples to it. The npm package exports a `sanitize(trace)` helper that strips message bodies before emission so teams cannot accidentally leak PII; PII safety is led on in the README, not buried in a bullet. Also exports an `agentgraph validate trace.json` CLI subcommand that checks a trace file against the schema and exits non-zero on violations — gives CI pipelines a hook with zero UI dependency. (3) A local-only CLI `agentgraph replay trace.json` that opens a pre-built static HTML file (no runtime bundler invocation, no local server) showing actual production paths overlaid on the declared machine; the trace file is loaded via drag-drop, rendered client-side, no backend, no ingest endpoint, no network calls. The replay UI ships as a standalone `agentgraph-devtools` package with its own version, decoupled from the core. README hero is a 90-second recorded terminal demo of a real agent trace rendering as a graph (recorded BEFORE the visualizer code is finalized as a forcing function). Hosted SaaS replay surface is a documented v2 milestone with a concrete adoption-signal threshold (e.g., >500 GitHub stars, >1000 weekly npm downloads) noted in the README so the v2 decision is not arbitrary.

proposals.title: ToolRoute
proposals.one_liner: A Vercel AI SDK companion that turns your tool definitions into a typed routing graph: each tool declares which tools may follow it, enforced at compile time AND at runtime with loud violations.
proposals.problem: The most common agent failure mode is not a bad state machine — it is tool ordering. The agent calls `send_email` before `draft_email`, or calls `commit` before `review`. Today you write this in the system prompt and pray. Vercel AI SDK gives you typed tools but no type-level way to express 'after `search`, the only legal next tools are `read` or `finish`'. ToolRoute wraps tool definitions so each tool carries a `nextAllowed` const tuple, and the SDK's tool-call type is narrowed per turn by the previous tool's output. The runtime guard rejects out-of-order calls in `strictMode`, so the type guarantee is operational, not decorative.
proposals.who_for: Solo devs and small teams building narrow vertical agents (sales-research bot, code-review bot, deploy-assistant) on Vercel AI SDK who want guarantees without learning XState. Theo-tier audience: 'just types, no runtime framework, no lock-in beyond the SDK they already use'.
proposals.mvp_scope: Single OSS npm package, ~500 LOC of pure types + a 50-line runtime guard. Exports `defineTool({ name, schema, nextAllowed: ['toolB', 'toolC'] as const })` and `createRouterFromTools([toolA, toolB])` — tool names are derived from the const tool array itself, so there is one single source of truth and renames cannot drift. The router's `tools` object handed to `streamText` is the standard Vercel AI SDK shape, but augmented so the union of permitted tools narrows per step based on the prior tool call. The runtime guard is built and demoed first (recorded terminal session showing the actual rejection); types are layered on top so the narrowing reflects observable runtime behavior, not a compile-time lie. `strictMode: true` throws on violation with a clear error including the offending tool, the previous tool, the legal next set, AND the SDK version the router was compiled against (`routerVersion` diagnostic field — first thing support will ask for when SDK internals drift). `strictMode: false` warns; an edge-runtime detection check surfaces a one-time initialization warning explaining that `console.warn` is suppressed in edge runtimes and rejections will throw, so users pipe runtime logs to capture violations. Adds a `printRouterGraph(router)` debug export that emits a plain-text adjacency list to stdout — zero deps, two minutes to implement, makes the routing graph inspectable without running the agent and patches the 'scattered nextAllowed' readability gap. Day-one README centerpiece: a complete three-tool code-review agent example (`search`, `review`, `commit`) with a recorded terminal session showing the red squiggle when you try to call `commit` before `review`, plus a side-by-side diff of raw Vercel AI SDK tool definition vs ToolRoute-wrapped version with the narrowed `nextAllowed` union called out. Cuts the live Cloudflare playground from v1 entirely (recovered as a v2 distraction-free milestone). Ships with `COMPATIBILITY.md` from day one — a public changelog of which Vercel AI SDK versions were tested, with a CI test against the latest SDK drop. README explicitly notes the Vercel AI SDK coupling as a known constraint and includes a rename-handling note (in the README, not discovered in a GitHub issue). No paid tier in v1 — keep it as a focused OSS calling card.
